planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil in deep shade to partial shade; space 18–24 inches apart and water regularly until established.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in well-drained, humus-rich soil in deep shade to partial shade; cast iron plant thrives in conditions that defeat nearly every other ornamental, including dense root competition under established trees, poor drainage, and near-zero indoor light. The broad, glossy, deep-green strap leaves grow slowly but are extraordinarily long-lived and require almost no maintenance beyond the occasional removal of damaged foliage. Water sparingly once established — the rhizomes store moisture and rot in permanently waterlogged soil — and fertilize lightly once or twice per year with a balanced slow-release fertilizer. Divide congested clumps in early spring every 5–10 years to maintain vigor.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring or early fall; lungwort is among the first shade perennials to bloom in late winter and requires consistently moist soil throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, spacing 12–18 inches apart; the silver-spotted leaves emerge before most shade perennials awaken and the funnel-shaped flowers that shift from pink buds to blue as they open make lungwort a standout in early spring. Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged — plants wilt dramatically in drought but recover quickly after watering. Shear the foliage to the ground in midsummer if it looks tired or shows powdery mildew; fresh leaves will emerge within weeks and carry the plant through autumn in good condition. Divide every 3–4 years in early fall when clumps begin to die out in the center.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Start seeds 12–14 weeks before desired transplant date by pressing them onto the surface of moist mix without covering, then cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks before moving to 50–60°F bright light; transplant into partial shade in fall or very early spring.

directions: Start seeds at cool temperatures (50–60°F) in moist seed-starting mix with seeds pressed on the surface; germination requires light and cool stratification, so refrigerate freshly sown trays at 35–40°F for three weeks before moving to a bright, cool location. Transplant into partially shaded, moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; primroses bloom prolifically in the cool dappled light under deciduous trees where spring sun reaches before the canopy leafs out. In zone 4–6 they are fully hardy perennials that return reliably each spring; in zone 7–8 treat them as cool-season annuals that perform best from fall through late spring and then go semi-dormant in summer heat. Divide overcrowded clumps in early fall every 2–3 years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of moist seed-starting mix without covering — browallia needs light to germinate — at 70–75°F; start 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to shaded beds after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist seed-starting mix without covering, as browallia needs light to germinate; maintain 70–75°F soil temperature and expect germination in 2–3 weeks. Transplant after last frost into well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial to full shade — it is one of the few true shade-loving annuals that blooms reliably without any direct sun, making it invaluable for north-facing beds, shaded patios, and hanging baskets under tree canopy. Pinch stems once or twice to encourage branching and a fuller mound of blue, violet, or white trumpet-shaped flowers, which bloom continuously from early summer until frost. Keep the soil consistently moist and water at the base to avoid fungal leaf spots.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Scatter seeds on moist prepared soil in fall or early spring, pressing lightly onto the surface; wild violet self-seeds prolifically and is best established by allowing naturally germinated seedlings to develop in place in moist, shaded locations.

directions: Scatter seeds directly on prepared moist soil in fall or early spring, pressing lightly onto the surface without burying; wild blue violet germinates best after a period of cool, moist stratification over winter. Plant in partial to full shade in moist, well-drained soil under trees or along woodland edges where it will naturalize freely by self-seeding and underground rhizome spread. Both the heart-shaped leaves and small purple-blue spring flowers are edible and have a long culinary history as salad greens, garnishes, and tea; harvest young spring leaves sparingly for the best mild flavor. This native groundcover is a critical early nectar source for pollinators and a required host plant for several native fritillary butterfly species in eastern North America.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; monkey grass tolerates a wide range of conditions and is best established by dividing existing clumps or planting rooted container stock.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun, spacing clumps 12–18 inches apart; monkey grass is one of the most reliable groundcovers for shaded areas under trees where lawn grass thins and fails. Established plants tolerate drought, poor soil, root competition, and deep shade with minimal care, and in zone 5–10 they require no supplemental irrigation once roots are established. The spikes of small violet-to-white flowers appear in late summer above the grassy foliage and are followed by round black berries that persist into winter, providing wildlife value. Shear or mow the entire planting back to 2–3 inches in late winter before new growth begins to refresh the appearance and remove winter-damaged tips.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant dormant bulbs in fall 2–3 inches deep and 6–9 inches apart in deep, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil under deciduous trees or shrubs; ramsons require a period of cold dormancy to initiate spring growth and should be mulched over winter in zone 4.

directions: Plant dormant bulbs in fall in deep, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees, setting them 2–3 inches deep and 6–9 inches apart; ramsons (wild garlic) is one of the most rewarding edible plants for the shade garden and one of the few alliums that actively thrives in heavy shade. The broad, bright-green leaves emerge in late winter or very early spring with a fresh garlic fragrance and can be harvested throughout the spring to use in salads, soups, pestos, and as a substitute for chives or wild garlic; the star-shaped white flower clusters are also edible and make attractive garnishes. After flowering in late spring the plant goes fully dormant by early summer — interplant with summer-growing hostas or ferns to fill the gap. Once established, ramsons spreads steadily to form a fragrant carpet that naturalizes under deciduous canopy.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–4 inches deep in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to filtered sun after soil warms above 60°F; white ginger lily thrives in dappled light under tall trees and along stream banks in zone 7–11.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 2–4 inches deep in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to filtered light in spring after soil warms above 60°F; white ginger lily is outstanding for Gulf Coast and zone 7–11 gardens where its intensely fragrant butterfly-white flowers perfume the garden in late summer and fall with one of the sweetest scents in the plant world. The tall, reed-like stems with broad tropical-looking leaves create bold textural interest from late spring through frost, making it an excellent specimen and a striking backdrop in mixed borders. In zone 7–8 the rhizomes may freeze to the ground in hard winters but re-emerge reliably from the roots in spring if protected with a thick mulch layer; in zone 9–11 it is evergreen. Divide congested clumps every 2–3 years in spring to maintain vigorous summer-to-fall flowering.

planting.note: Root leaf sections or offset divisions in well-drained cactus or succulent mix at 70–80°F in bright indirect to low light; in zone 9–12 it may be planted outdoors year-round in very well-drained soil in deep to partial shade.

directions: Root leaf cuttings or offset divisions in well-drained cactus mix in bright indirect light to very low light; the snake plant (formerly Sansevieria trifasciata, reclassified to Dracaena in 2017) is one of the most forgiving indoor plants available, surviving weeks of neglect, poor light, and inconsistent watering that defeat most other houseplants. Water sparingly — no more than once every 2–3 weeks in winter and once a week in summer — allowing the soil to dry completely between waterings; root rot from overwatering is by far the most common cause of decline. In zone 9–12 it can be grown as an outdoor landscape plant in full shade to partial shade in very well-drained soil, where it forms bold architectural clumps of stiff, upright, green-and-yellow banded foliage to 4 feet tall. Note that the leaves are mildly toxic to cats and dogs if ingested.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in late summer to early fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained soil; seedlings overwinter as rosettes and bolt to bloom in spring of the following year. In mild climates seeds can also be sown in early spring for some first-year bloom.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in late summer or early fall in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; seedlings overwinter as flat rosettes before bolting and blooming in spring of the second year with fragrant clusters of four-petaled purple or white flowers that attract early spring pollinators. After flowering, the large flat oval seedpods develop and ripen to translucent silver-white discs that persist on the stems through autumn and are highly prized in dried arrangements under the common name 'silver dollar plant.' Once established, honesty self-seeds prolifically and reappears each year without deliberate replanting, behaving in effect as a naturalized perennial in most gardens. Thin seedlings to 9–12 inches apart and allow some pods to shatter naturally to perpetuate the colony indefinitely.
